title: Modi at G7 Evian: India Charts Inclusive Growth Model Against Western Economic Orthodoxy
lede: Prime Minister Narendra Modi addressed the G7 Summit in Evian on sustainable economic growth, presenting India's inclusive development philosophy as an alternative framework for global prosperity amid mounting uncertainties.

sections.context: Prime Minister Modi participated in an Outreach Session at the G7 Summit in Evian, France on 17 June 2026, focused on 'Reviving a Balanced, Shared and Sustainable Economic Growth for All.' India's participation in this traditionally Western-led forum reflects the broader shift toward inclusive multilateralism. Modi's intervention targeted a fundamental premise of G7 economics: that growth measured purely through GDP and trade metrics constitutes adequate development policy. He reframed the conversation explicitly around real-world inclusion and human well-being, positioning India's homegrown framework of 'Sabka Sath, Sabka Vikas, Sabka Vishwas, Sabka Prayas' (Together with all, Development for all, Trust of all, Effort of all) as a more holistic alternative to narrow GDP-focused models at a moment when global uncertainties demand fresh thinking.
sections.threads: This intervention extends India's intellectual leadership demonstrated during its G20 presidency, where the organizing motto 'One Earth, One Family, One Future' similarly elevated collective welfare over competitive accumulation. Modi's framing at Evian directly operationalizes that philosophy within Western institutional spaces, challenging G7 members to adopt broader development metrics. The strategic anchoring of India's international engagements—from multilateral forums to bilateral infrastructure projects—around inclusive growth principles suggests a coordinated diplomatic effort. The India Middle East Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC), referenced in Modi's remarks as embodying this inclusive approach, represents the practical institutional expression of this worldview, connecting three geopolitically significant regions through development logic that prioritizes shared benefits over extractive partnerships.
sections.signal: Watch for G7 member responses to India's inclusive growth framework in coming communiqués and policy adjustments. The critical juncture lies in whether Western economies integrate India's well-being-centered metrics into their climate finance, development assistance, and trade agendas over the next 12-18 months. Monitor how India's IMEC operationalization—particularly infrastructure timelines and equity distribution mechanisms—either validates or undermines the inclusive growth rhetoric Modi articulated at Evian. Parallels with bilateral Franco-Indian strategic elevation frameworks will indicate whether India is winning institutional legitimacy for its model within traditional Western partnership structures. The acid test: whether sustainable growth conversations at the next G7 or G20 summit explicitly embed inclusion and trust-building as measurable outcomes rather than rhetorical add-ons.
